questions.prompt: DNSFilter sub-orgs (child organisations) are populated with an MSP partner account type selected at signup; you can't retrofit multi-tenancy after the fact without DNSFilter's help.
questions.explanation: The MSP partner account type is selected at signup. Migrating from a single-Organization account to a partner account is not a self-service operation.

questions.prompt: A user with Super Admin on Customer A's sub-org can also see Customer B's policies and Query Log because they share the same MSP parent.
questions.explanation: Sub-org scope is hard isolation. A Super Admin on one sub-org has no visibility into siblings. The MSP-layer admins are the only ones with cross-customer visibility, and that's why customer-side users are scoped to their sub-org only.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are real RBAC anti-patterns to avoid? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A single shared MSP credential used by all techs
Per-tech accounts with SSO via the MSP IdP
A customer-side IT lead granted Super Admin at the MSP layer
An Owner role assigned to a service account with documented handover
An Owner role held by 'whoever signed up first', no documentation
questions.explanation: Shared credentials destroy the audit trail. Customer admins at the MSP layer cross the partner-access boundary. Forever-Owner with no documentation is a hostage scenario. Per-tech SSO and Owner-as-service-account are correct patterns.

questions.prompt: An MSP wants 'which of our customers got hit hardest by the campaign last week' across the entire customer book. Which surface delivers that?
choices: Per-sub-org Insights, manually summed
The Overview dashboard
Data Export add-on aggregated into a central data lake
AppAware Reporting
questions.explanation: Cross-sub-org aggregation isn't natively presented in DNSFilter. The Data Export add-on flowed into a central data lake (or queried via the API) is the right path.

questions.prompt: Per-user reporting in DNSFilter requires the Users feature to be enabled and populated.
questions.explanation: Without the Users feature, drill-down stops at Site or Roaming Client. Users is also what enables per-user policy assignment; you usually want it on for any customer with more than a small handful of staff.

questions.prompt: DNSFilter SSO is built on which protocol?
choices: SAML 2.0
OpenID Connect (OIDC)
OAuth 2.0 alone
Kerberos
questions.explanation: DNSFilter SSO uses OIDC and works with any IdP that supports the generic OIDC flow. There are dedicated guides for Entra, Okta, Google Workspace, and Active Directory.

questions.prompt: DNSFilter's documented recovery pattern when SSO keys expire or an account is locked out is to:
choices: Wait for DNSFilter support to disable SSO at the platform level
Assign at least one Super Admin role to a shared email so the team has a documented way back in
Pre-generate emergency codes during SSO setup
Configure every user with a password fallback alongside SSO
questions.explanation: DNSFilter explicitly recommends assigning at least one Super Admin role to a shared email so the organisation has a way back in if SSO keys expire or accounts are locked out. That's the escape hatch, not a per-user password fallback for everyone.

questions.prompt: DNSFilter API JWTs (tokens) are shown only once when generated; the Save button doesn't activate until you've copied the value.
questions.explanation: DNSFilter's documentation is explicit about this. Miss the copy, regenerate. Long-lived JWTs in the wrong place are a credential-leak class incident.

questions.prompt: Which of these are DNSFilter's named integration partners? (Select all that apply.)
choices: HaloPSA
Syncro
ConnectWise PSA
Atera
Autotask
Pulseway PSA
Zapier
ServiceNow ITOM
questions.explanation: DNSFilter publishes integration guides for six PSAs (HaloPSA, Syncro, ConnectWise PSA, Atera, Autotask, Pulseway PSA) plus Zapier for automation. ServiceNow ITOM is not on the documented integrations list.

questions.prompt: Which integrations should an MSP NOT build? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A webhook from DNSFilter into the PSA on threat-block events
An auto-allowlist endpoint where users self-serve unblock requests
A SIEM forwarding pipeline via Data Export
A real-time block-notification email per blocked query to the user
An IP-block mirror in the firewall for every domain DNSFilter blocks
questions.explanation: Self-service unblock is a security regression, users can't tell phishing from legitimate. Per-block emails ticket your own helpdesk into oblivion. IP-mirroring DNS blocks fails on shared CDN IPs and fast-flux. Webhooks into PSA and SIEM forwarding are good patterns.

questions.prompt: Put the three Query Log pivots in the right investigation order.
items: Other users on the same domain, campaign or single-user shape
Full activity for the affected user/device, proximity events and timing patterns
Domain reputation and history, confirm the verdict against external sources
questions.explanation: Scope first (how many users), then character of the activity (proximity events, timing), then external confirmation. Each pivot answers a question the next one builds on.

questions.prompt: Which questions can DNSFilter's Query Log NOT answer on its own? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Did this user click the link in the phishing email?
Did this user enter credentials on the phishing page before the redirect was blocked?
What domain did this device query at 14:32?
Was a session token captured by the attacker?
How many users in the org were targeted by the campaign?
questions.explanation: The Query Log sees DNS queries only, not clicks, not form submissions, not session-token capture. Those questions belong to email security, the IdP audit log, and EDR. Your write-up should hand those questions off to the right tool, not pretend the Query Log answered them.

questions.prompt: Put the DNSFilter customer-exit cleanup in the safest order.
items: Export reports, deployment inventory, and policy evidence
Uninstall Roaming Clients through supported device paths
Clean stale Users and Roaming Client records
Remove dashboard access, SSO exceptions, and API keys
Review customer-specific lists, integrations, and billing handoff
questions.explanation: Preserve evidence first. Remove live agents before deleting records. Then close access, lists, integrations, and billing evidence.
